questions: Should 406 conflict recovery merge the server's config with local edits, or is pure last-write-wins (local state always overwrites server) acceptable? The current code and idea description suggest LWW, but if AI agents make structural changes (e.g., adding tracks) that the user hasn't seen, LWW will silently discard them.

Is there a test suite for the video editor save logic (unit or integration)? The explore didn't find one — if tests exist, the plan should include updating them. If not, should we add tests for the retry logic?

The polling interval is 30s. If all 3 retries fail, the user waits up to 30s for polling to fix the version. Is that acceptable, or should we reduce the poll interval after a conflict (e.g., poll immediately after exhausting retries)?
success_criteria: After the fix, rapidly moving clips in the timeline never results in the save status getting stuck on 'error' — it always recovers to 'saved' within a few seconds.
Two concurrent writers (two tabs, or user + AI agent) on the same timeline both eventually reach 'saved' status without manual intervention.
After a 406 conflict, `shouldAcceptPolledData` is not permanently blocked — polling resumes and can sync external changes.
No overlapping `saveTimeline` calls in flight — the serialization guard ensures at most one save RPC is active at a time.
The save debounce (500ms) still works correctly for normal editing — rapid keystrokes are batched, not sent individually.
assumptions: Last-write-wins is the desired semantics — when a 406 occurs, we refresh the version and re-save our local state, overwriting whatever the server has. We do NOT attempt three-way merge.

MAX_CONFLICT_RETRIES = 3 is sufficient. In practice, conflicts should resolve on the first retry since we fetch the latest version. Multiple retries only matter if a third writer is also saving simultaneously.
The SQL RPC does not need changes. The server-side optimistic locking is correct; all fixes are client-side.

The `saveMutation` from react-query can be called re-entrantly (via `mutateAsync`) — we handle serialization ourselves via `isSavingRef` rather than relying on react-query's built-in mutation state, because we need fine-grained control over retry behavior.
Debug logging can use bare `console.log` with `[TimelineSave]` tags per the project's debugging convention.
